title.zh: 第一回 桃林結義 玄圭出世
title.en: Chapter 1: Brotherhood Sworn in the Peach Grove; the Dark Omen Comes into the World

en: Peach blossoms, blood-red, bind three sworn souls
The dark jade gleams cold across the Nine Furnaces

passages.zh: 話說九爐之世，天地猶未定形，諸侯割據，各擅山川之利。鐵城永邑居九爐之中，城牆高可摩雲，爐火晝夜不熄，鑄甲三千，威震四方。其主吳烈，身長九尺，面如玄鐵，目若炬火，自幼從征，未嘗一敗。世人皆稱之曰：「永邑之虎，天下無雙。」然盛名之下，民心未必盡服。是歲夏末，城中糧賦加重，徭役不休，市井之間，怨聲隱隱如地底悶雷，積而待發。
passages.en: In the age of the Nine Furnaces, when heaven and earth had not yet settled into fixed form, the lords of the realm divided the mountains and rivers among themselves, each grasping what advantage the land afforded. The iron city of Yong stood at the heart of the Nine Furnaces: its walls rose high enough to graze the clouds, its furnace-fires burned without ceasing through day and night, it had forged three thousand suits of armour, and its might awed the four quarters. Its lord, Wu Lie, stood nine feet tall, his face dark as cast iron, his eyes bright as torchfire. From childhood he had gone on campaigns, and he had never once been defeated. The people of the world called him thus: 'The tiger of Yong — without equal under heaven.' Yet beneath such renown, the hearts of the common people were not entirely won. In the last weeks of that summer, the grain levies in the city had been doubled, corvée labour went without rest, and through the lanes and markets a murmur of resentment gathered — like thunder suppressed beneath the earth, accumulating, waiting to break.

passages.zh: 一日，東市徵糧官強奪寡婦陳氏之粟，婦人哭號於街，旁觀者皆側目而不敢言。忽一壯漢自人群中躍出，身著獸皮，髮如亂草，赤足踏地，聲若洪鐘，喝道：「官府橫行，天理何在！」一拳打翻徵糧小吏，眾人嘩然。此人乃璟同，野生之士，自幼長於永邑城外雲嵐山中，與獸為伍，不識禮法，然天生神力，義膽包天。消息傳至吳烈耳中，烈大怒，親率甲士趕赴東市，欲拿此人問罪。
passages.en: One day, a grain-collection officer in the Eastern Market seized by force the millet of a widow named Chen. The woman wailed in the street; bystanders averted their eyes and dared not speak. Suddenly a powerfully built man leapt from the crowd. He wore hides of wild animals; his hair was tangled as weeds; he went barefoot on the earth; and his voice rang out like a great bell: 'When officials run rampant, where does Heaven's reason go?' With one fist he knocked the grain clerk flat. The crowd erupted in uproar. This man was Jing Tong — a wild-born warrior, raised since childhood in the Cloud-Mist Mountains outside the walls of Yong, companion to beasts, ignorant of ritual propriety, yet endowed from birth with godlike strength and a righteousness vast as the sky. Word reached Wu Lie's ears, and Wu Lie was enraged. He led armoured soldiers personally to the Eastern Market, intending to seize this man and call him to account.

zh: 鐵蹄踏碎青石街，殺氣橫掃市中天。
野人立地如山嶽，虎目圓睜不肯遷。
二虎相逢爭王氣，長街盡頭是血緣。
en: Iron hooves shatter the cobblestone street; a killing air sweeps the sky above the market.
The wild man stands upon the earth like a mountain, tiger-eyes wide open, refusing to yield.
Two tigers meet and contend for kingly breath — at the far end of the long street, blood ties wait.

passages.zh: 吳烈至，見璟同獨立塵埃之中，神色從容，四周甲士已將其圍困。烈喝令拿下，璟同卻仰天長笑，答曰：「天下英雄，我未嘗服過一人。汝既是永邑之主，且來較量。」吳烈聞言，怒火中燒，然又隱覺此人氣度非凡，乃摒退左右，親自下馬，以拳脚相搏。兩人廝打半日，街石皆碎，屋瓦俱震。吳烈一生征戰，從未遭逢對手，此番竟與一野人打成平手。日暮時分，二人皆立而喘，互視良久，相顧大笑。吳烈心中某處忽然鬆動，如久封之閘門，洪水欲奔。
passages.en: Wu Lie arrived to find Jing Tong standing alone amid the dust, his bearing composed, surrounded already by armoured soldiers on all sides. Wu Lie shouted the order to seize him; but Jing Tong threw back his head and laughed aloud at the sky, answering: 'Among all the heroes under heaven, I have yet to submit to a single one. Since you are the lord of Yong, come and let us measure ourselves.' Hearing this, Wu Lie's anger blazed up — yet he sensed at once that this man's bearing was extraordinary. He waved his soldiers back, dismounted himself, and they fought with fists and feet. The two men grappled for half a day; the paving stones cracked, the roof-tiles shook. Wu Lie had fought wars his entire life and never encountered a match — yet now, against a wild-born man, he had been fought to a standstill. At dusk the two stood panting, facing each other for a long moment, then looked at one another and burst out laughing together. Something in Wu Lie's chest suddenly loosened, as a floodgate long sealed gives way before onrushing water.

redComment.zh: 此一「鬆動」二字，寫盡英雄遇知己之微妙。鐵石心腸，逢璟同而始柔，此後悲劇之根，已種於此笑中矣。批者淚下。
redComment.en: These two characters — 'loosened' — capture perfectly the subtle tremor of a hero meeting his true companion. An iron heart softens only upon meeting Jing Tong; the seeds of all later tragedy are already planted within this laugh. The commentator's tears fall.

passages.zh: 自此，吳烈不再問璟同之罪，反延之入府，以上賓待之。然府中食衣皆有規制，璟同百般不慣，常赤膊奔走庭院，引得侍從掩口而笑。軍師昭明者，永邑謀主也，面白如玉，目光深遠如秋水。昭明初見璟同，撫掌曰：「此天降之將，非俗世所能羈束，主公得此人，如虎添翼矣。」三人相處日久，情義漸深，吳烈嘗於獨處時歎曰：「吾征戰半生，朋友無幾，孰知於一街市之鬥中，得此二人。」
passages.en: From that day forward, Wu Lie no longer sought to punish Jing Tong; instead he invited him into the manor and received him as a guest of honour. Yet the manor's food and clothing all followed prescribed forms, and Jing Tong found nothing to his liking. He would often run through the courtyards bare-chested, causing the attendants to cover their mouths and laugh. The strategist Zhao Ming, master counsellor of Yong, had a face white as jade and eyes of deep and distant clarity, like autumn water. When Zhao Ming first laid eyes on Jing Tong, he clapped his hands together and said: 'This is a general sent down by Heaven — no ordinary world can bind him. My lord, in gaining this man, you are a tiger given wings.' As the three men spent more days together, the bonds of feeling and loyalty deepened. Wu Lie would sometimes sigh in private moments: 'Half my life I have fought wars and had few friends — who could have known that in a street-market brawl I would find these two men?'

passages.zh: 是年中秋之後，永邑城西三十里有桃林一片，桃樹數百株，秋意已深，葉落殆盡，唯枯枝橫斜，然其間時有晚桃數朵，殘紅猶艷，如血珠綴於銀枝之上。吳烈召昭明、璟同至此，設牛酒，鋪黃土，三人跪伏，各刺指血，滴於同一盞酒中，仰天盟誓。昭明代三人立誓詞，辭曰：「吾三人雖非同年同月同日生，但願同心共力，扶危救困，不負天地，不負此生。有渝此誓，天地共殛之。」誓畢，三人飲血酒，擲盞於地，盞碎聲中，彼此相視，淚光隱現。
passages.en: After the Mid-Autumn of that year, thirty li west of the city of Yong there lay a grove of peach trees — several hundred trees in all. The autumn had already deepened, the leaves had mostly fallen, and only bare branches crossed the air at angles; yet here and there late peach blossoms still opened in ones and twos, their fading crimson still vivid, like drops of blood strung upon silver branches. Wu Lie summoned Zhao Ming and Jing Tong to this place. He set out an ox and wine, spread yellow earth upon the ground, and the three men knelt and bowed. Each pricked a finger, letting blood fall into the same cup of wine. They lifted their faces to heaven and swore an oath. Zhao Ming composed the vow on behalf of all three: 'Though we three were not born in the same year, the same month, or the same day, we vow to be of one mind and united strength — to support the endangered, to relieve the suffering, never to betray heaven and earth, never to betray this life. Should any of us break this oath, let heaven and earth together strike him down.' The oath was spoken; the three men drank the blood-wine; they flung the cup to the ground; in the crack of its shattering they looked at one another, and a glimmer of tears showed in every eye.

zh: 殘桃幾朵綴枯枝，血酒同飲誓難移。
昭明執筆書盟約，璟同仰天淚不知。
吳烈此生多鐵骨，今日方識柔腸時。
桃林風起花如雨，三魂從此結一絲。
en: A few spent blossoms cling to bare branches; blood-wine drunk together — the vow will not shift.
Zhao Ming takes the brush and writes the covenant; Jing Tong throws back his face to heaven, unaware of tears.
Wu Lie's life has been all iron and bone — only today does he learn the hour when tenderness comes.
Wind rises in the peach grove and blossoms fall like rain; three souls are from this moment bound by a single thread.

redComment.zh: 「一絲」者，情之細而難斷也。作者於此用「絲」而不用「繩」，蓋絲者易斷而斷之最痛，一字藏盡後文璟同之死與吳烈之哭。讀者不可輕過。
redComment.en: The word 'thread' — fine yet unbreakable in feeling. The author here writes 'thread' rather than 'rope': a thread is easily severed, and yet its severing is the most agonising. In this single word the death of Jing Tong and the weeping of Wu Lie are entirely concealed. The reader must not pass lightly over this.

passages.zh: 永邑之東，有苑名曰朱華苑，乃吳烈命人廣植花木所築，曲廊迴環，池水清澈，四時花開不絕。苑中最奇者，乃一株古梅，樹齡逾三百年，幹若虯龍，花時香氣可傳十里。吳烈每於征戰歸來，必先入此苑，獨坐片刻，方能重歸人間之態。苑中有一女，名喚雲素華，乃孤女出身，自幼入苑，工詩善書，性情幽靜如深水。她每日持筆記錄苑中一切，花開幾朵，葉落幾片，偶有外間消息傳入，亦一一錄於簿冊之中。眾人皆知其名，然見其人者少，如苑中那株古梅，默然自存。
passages.en: To the east of Yong lay a garden known as the Vermilion Garden, which Wu Lie had ordered planted with broad expanses of flowers and trees. Winding covered walkways circled in on themselves; the pool water ran clear; flowers opened without ceasing through all four seasons. The most wondrous thing in the garden was an ancient plum tree, more than three hundred years old, its trunk twisted like a coiling dragon; when it bloomed its fragrance could carry ten li. Every time Wu Lie returned from a campaign, he would first enter this garden and sit alone for a time before he could resume a human bearing. Within the garden there lived a young woman named Yun Suhua — of orphaned origin, she had entered the garden from childhood, excelling in poetry and accomplished in calligraphy, her temperament still and deep as a well. Each day she took up her brush and recorded everything in the garden: how many flowers had opened, how many leaves had fallen; and when occasional news from the outside world reached her, she recorded that too, item by item, in her ledgers. Everyone in the manor knew her name, yet few had actually seen her — like that ancient plum tree in the garden, she existed in silent self-containment.

passages.zh: 桃林結義之日，雲素華身在朱華苑，自不曾親睹，然訊息傳回之時，她手中筆不曾停，於簿中寫下數行。其字跡細如蚊足，然字字清晰如刀刻。她寫道：「是日，城西桃林，三人結義。一為永邑之主，剛烈如火；一為謀士，冷靜如冰；一為野人，渾然如山。火與山相逢，城不燃而更危，此非佳兆也。吾恐此後，朱華苑中，歡笑漸少，墨痕漸多。」書罷，擱筆，窗外梅花忽落一瓣，無風自墮，輕飄飄落入硯中，染墨半片，如淚痕。
passages.en: On the day the oath was sworn in the peach grove, Yun Suhua was in the Vermilion Garden and had not witnessed it herself; yet when news was carried back, her brush in hand never paused. She wrote several lines in her ledger. Her characters were thin as mosquito-feet, yet each one was clear as if cut by a knife. She wrote: 'On this day, in the peach grove west of the city, three men swore brotherhood. One is the lord of Yong, fierce and ardent as fire; one is a counsellor, still and cool as ice; one is a wild-born man, whole and uncut as a mountain. When fire meets mountain, the city does not burn — and yet the danger is greater. This is not a good omen. I fear that hereafter, within the Vermilion Garden, laughter will grow fewer and traces of ink more numerous.' When she had finished writing, she set down her brush. Outside the window a plum petal fell — falling of itself without wind — and drifted down into the inkstone, staining half of it with ink, like the track of a tear.

redComment.anchor: 火與山相逢，城不燃而更危
redComment.zh: 素華此語，是全書之眼也。火烈者吳烈，山渾者璟同，二人相合不相毀，其力反大，故禍更深。作者借女子之筆，道破天機，而女子獨知，男兒不聞，此世間常態，令人哀也。
redComment.en: These words of Suhua are the eye of the entire book. Wu Lie is the ardent fire; Jing Tong is the whole mountain. When two such forces unite without destroying each other, their combined power is greater still — and therefore the catastrophe runs deeper. The author uses a woman's brush to pierce through to heaven's secret, yet the woman alone perceives it while the men hear nothing. This is the common condition of the world — and cause enough for grief.

zh: 素華獨坐窗前夜，燈影搖搖字亦斜。
苑外歡聲傳義氣，苑中墨淚潤梅花。
en: Suhua sits alone before her window in the night; the lamp's shadow wavers, and her characters lean.
Outside the garden, sounds of joy carry the breath of brotherhood; within, ink-tears moisten the plum blossoms.

passages.zh: 卻說九爐之世，除諸侯紛爭之外，另有一物，乃天下覬覦之所在，名曰天命璽。此璽以崑崙玄玉所制，通體幽黑，刻山河萬里之紋，持之者如握天下之重，輕則三百斤。璽本供奉於九爐居中之靈嶽——玄樞山之頂，由三族共守，號稱中立之物，諸侯無論勝負，皆不得擅取。然璽之存在，本身即是禍因，天下人皆知持璽者天命所歸，故覬覦之心，百年不絕。是時玄樞山守璽之三族，已因瑣事生隙，各懷疑心，守備漸弛。吳烈聞之，曾密告昭明曰：「璽若易主，天下必亂。」昭明撚須沉思，良久曰：「亂，本已在途中。」
passages.en: Yet in the age of the Nine Furnaces, beyond the struggles of the lords, there existed one object that all the world coveted: the Jade Seal of Heaven's Mandate. This seal had been fashioned from the dark jade of the Kunlun Mountains. Its entire body was of a deep, still black, engraved with the patterns of mountains and rivers for ten thousand li. Whoever held it held what felt like the full weight of the world in their hands — no lighter than three hundred catties. The Seal had been enshrined at the summit of Xuanshu Mountain — the Spirit Peak at the heart of the Nine Furnaces — jointly guarded by three clans, proclaimed a neutral object: whatever the outcome of any lord's victory or defeat, none might seize it unilaterally. Yet the very existence of the Seal was itself a cause of calamity, for all under heaven knew that whoever held the Seal had heaven's mandate conferred upon them; and so the desire to possess it had gone unextinguished for a hundred years. At this time the three clans guarding the Seal on Xuanshu Mountain had already fallen into friction over minor matters, each harbouring suspicion of the others, and their vigilance had gradually slackened. Wu Lie, hearing of this, once privately informed Zhao Ming: 'If the Seal changes hands, the realm will certainly fall into chaos.' Zhao Ming stroked his beard in deep thought, and after a long silence said: 'Chaos — is already on its way.'

passages.zh: 朱華苑之設，原為吳烈平生難得之溫柔鄉，苑中有荷池、梅塢、竹徑、菊圃，四時景致各異。璟同初入苑中，見滿目繁花，竟呆立半晌，喃喃曰：「我在山中二十年，從未見此物。」吳烈笑道：「此後苑便是你的，想何時來便何時來。」璟同聞言，赧然低頭，指尖輕觸一朵半開的朱槿，輕聲道：「如此嬌弱，真不知如何是好。」吳烈立於其側，望之良久，心中隱生一種從未有過的暖意，如冬日爐火，悄悄映亮一室。
passages.en: The Vermilion Garden had been established as a rare and cherished refuge of tenderness in Wu Lie's life. Within it were a lotus pond, a plum-tree hollow, bamboo-lined paths, and chrysanthemum beds, each season offering its own distinct scene. The first time Jing Tong entered the garden, he stood motionless for a long while at the sight of flowers filling his view on every side, murmuring: 'I lived in the mountains for twenty years and never saw such things.' Wu Lie laughed and said: 'From now on the garden is yours — come whenever you please.' Hearing this, Jing Tong ducked his head, a flush of feeling in his face, and touched the tip of one finger to a half-open red hibiscus blossom, saying softly: 'Something so delicate — I truly don't know how to treat it.' Wu Lie stood at his side and watched him for a long moment, and in his heart there grew, quietly, a warmth he had never felt before — like a winter hearth-fire, gently illuminating an entire room.

zh: 鐵人入苑識芳菲，低首輕觸朱槿枝。
一世殺伐不知柔，今朝苑中始學遲。
吳烈旁觀心已亂，此暖從來不知歸。
en: An iron man enters the garden and learns what fragrance is; he bows his head to touch the red hibiscus branch.
A lifetime of killing and war, never knowing softness — only now, in the garden, does he begin to learn, and slowly.
Wu Lie watches from the side, his heart already unsettled; this warmth — he has never known before where it might go.

passages.zh: 是夜，雲素華於燈下覽舊簿，見往年所錄苑事，字字如昨，心中感慨，又提筆添錄今日之事：「璟同入苑，如山入水，兩相映照，竟成奇景。吳烈性烈，璟同性野，然二者相處，竟有難言之默契，如天成。昭明在旁，面有微笑，然笑中有算，算中有憂，憂而不言。吾今日見此三人，忽想起幼時所讀古語：二火相遇，不燃則熄。二人皆是火，惟不知燃者何物，熄者何時。苑中梅樹今夜落瓣尤多，不知何故，拾得數瓣，置於書中留存。」記畢，合簿，燈花爆裂，室中一暗，素華對影獨坐，心中隱有所感，難以名狀。
passages.en: That night, Yun Suhua reviewed old ledgers by lamplight, reading what had been recorded in past years about the garden's affairs — each character fresh as yesterday — and feeling much move within her. She lifted her brush again and added the day's entry: 'Jing Tong entered the garden today, as a mountain enters water; the two reflecting each other formed a strange and striking scene. Wu Lie's nature is fierce; Jing Tong's nature is wild; yet the two of them together possess an unspoken accord that seems natural as if heaven-made. Zhao Ming stood to the side with a slight smile on his face — yet within the smile there was calculation, and within the calculation, anxiety; anxious, and yet saying nothing. Seeing these three men today, I suddenly recalled an old saying I read in childhood: Two fires when they meet will either flare up or go out. Both men are fire; I only do not know what it is that will burn, nor when it will be extinguished. The plum tree in the garden shed an unusually large number of petals tonight — I do not know why. I gathered several and placed them in the book for safekeeping.' When the entry was complete, she closed the ledger. The lamp-flower burst and crackled; the room dimmed for a moment. Suhua sat alone facing her own shadow, with something stirring in her heart that had no name.

zh: 燈前合簿夜已深，梅瓣書中藏幾春。
素華默坐無言語，紙上英雄紙上人。
明日戰鼓或又響，苑中誰解此時心。
en: Before the lamp she closes the ledger; the night has deepened; plum petals tucked in the book hold how many springs.
Suhua sits silent with no words to speak — heroes on paper, people on paper.
Tomorrow the war-drums may sound again; within the garden, who could understand this moment's heart.

passages.zh: 欲知後事如何，且看下回分解。天命璽既存於世，玄樞山守備日弛，而諸侯之野心，從未一日止息。朱華苑中，三人義氣初結，笑語猶溫，然素華筆下之憂，已如伏流潛行於地。世事之轉，往往不在鐘鼓齊鳴之時，而在一枚花瓣無聲落入硯中之際。讀者諸君，且將此一笑、此一淚、此一瓣梅花，一齊收入心底，以待後章之驗。正是：兩火相遇，城未燃而憂先至；三義初成，苑尤在而人將散。
passages.en: As for what happened next, the following chapter will reveal all. The Jade Seal of Heaven's Mandate remained in the world; the defences of Xuanshu Mountain grew slacker day by day; and the ambitions of the lords had never ceased for a single day. Within the Vermilion Garden, the bonds of brotherhood among the three men had just been forged, their laughter and warmth still fresh — yet the anxiety traced by Suhua's brush already moved like an underground current flowing unseen beneath the earth. The turning of worldly affairs so often comes not in the moment of drums and bells sounding together, but in the instant when a single petal falls in silence into an inkstone. Gentle readers: take this one laugh, this one tear, this one plum petal, and gather them all into the depths of your heart, to await their verification in the chapters to come. As it truly is: Two fires meet — the city has not yet burned, yet sorrow arrives first. The three-part brotherhood is newly formed — the garden still stands, yet the people will soon be scattered.
